title: Febrile Seizures
objectives: Distinguish simple from complex febrile seizures
Counsel families on recurrence risk and prognosis
Recognize when anticonvulsant therapy is indicated

vignette.text:
An 18-month-old boy is brought to the ED after a witnessed seizure at home. His mother reports he had 2 days of rhinorrhea and cough, then developed fever to 103°F this morning. Shortly after, he had a generalized shaking episode lasting ~2 minutes with post-ictal drowsiness. He is now alert and interactive.

Past Medical History: Full-term, normal development, no prior seizures
Vaccinations: Up to date
Family History: Father had febrile seizures as a child

physicalExam.HEENT: TMs clear bilaterally, pharynx mildly erythematous, rhinorrhea
physicalExam.neurologic: Alert, normal tone, moving all extremities equally, no meningismus

questions.question: Which features define a SIMPLE febrile seizure?
options: Generalized, <15 min, single episode in 24h, age 6mo-5yr
questions.explanation: Simple febrile seizure: generalized, <15 minutes, does not recur within 24 hours, in child 6 months to 5 years without underlying neurologic disease.

questions.question: This child's seizure is classified as:
questions.explanation: This was generalized, lasted 2 minutes (<15), single episode, in appropriate age range with fever from viral URI = simple febrile seizure.

questions.question: When is LP indicated for febrile seizure?
options: Signs of meningitis or unvaccinated/partially vaccinated child
questions.explanation: LP is indicated when there are signs of meningitis (meningismus, toxic appearance, prolonged AMS) or in unvaccinated/partially vaccinated children where meningitis cannot be clinically excluded.

questions.question: What is the recurrence risk after a first simple febrile seizure?
questions.explanation: Recurrence risk is ~30-35% overall. Risk factors for recurrence: young age at first seizure (<15 months), family history, low-grade fever at seizure, brief duration of fever before seizure.

questions.question: What should parents be told about epilepsy risk?
options: Risk is slightly elevated (~2-4%) vs general population (1%)
questions.explanation: Simple febrile seizures carry only slightly increased epilepsy risk (2-4% vs 1% general population). Complex febrile seizures have higher risk (~6-8%). Prophylactic anticonvulsants do NOT prevent later epilepsy.

keyTakeaways: Simple FS: generalized, <15 min, single in 24h, age 6mo-5yr
Complex FS: focal, >15 min, recurrent in 24h, or abnormal neuro exam
LP if signs of meningitis or inadequate vaccination
Recurrence ~30-35%; epilepsy risk only slightly elevated
